THE COPYWRITER BLUEPRINT
Homework Hot Seats
Week 2, Session 3

-- 5 of 20 --

THE POWER OF SELF-IDENTIFYING AVATARS
‣ Yes, that’s a branded term. You’ll get a DOSE of them. : )
Extra credit tip: brand ‘everything’ you can.
‣ 	What is an Avatar?
An acid-trip movie…and more to our point, the person or group
you’re writing to. We’ll cover examples on the next slide.
‣ What does “self-identifying” mean?
Self-identifying avatars are labels that your prospect either already
identifies with OR wishes to identify with.
‣ What’s the BIG WIN here?
You create FANS. You speak to a 	MOVEMENT.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 20 --

WHAT MAKES A GREAT AVATAR?
‣ Short
1-3 words max; one word is always best, although two is often needed
‣ 	Descriptive
As the name implies, something they identify with and would used
readily to describe themselves.
‣ For The Big Win:
A moniker they would 	love to use for themselves. 	You want
something that makes someone go, “Hmmm…I like the way that
makes me sound!”
‣ Wile E. Coyotee… 	SUPER-GENIUS!
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 20 --

THE THREE TIERS OF AVATARS
‣ 	Tier 1: The “This Sucks” Tier
These are typical “avatars”. They’re actually “demographics”.
Important, but many copywriters get them confused:
‣ menopausal women over 50 	≠ 	avatar
‣ people who want to lose weight 	≠ 	avatar
‣ 	people who spend more than $1000/day on Facebook 	≠ 	avatar
‣ frequent air travelers 	≠ 	avatar
‣ These are demographics (important, but not avatars)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 20 --

THE THREE TIERS OF AVATARS
‣ Tier 2: The “Pretty Damn Good Tier”
These are mostly 	previously 	self-identifying — meaning they carry
this label already, but it’s not “kickass secret society” level avatars…
‣ menopausal women over 50: “menopause survivors” (humor),
“retiring mothers” (assumption)
‣ 	Men over 35 who want to lose weight: “dieters”, “healthy
eaters” (moving toward Tier 3) – notice I don’t have to say
“men”?
‣ people who spend more than $1000/day on Facebook:
“marketers”, “ad managers”
‣ frequent air travelers: “flyers”, “frequent flyers”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 20 --

THE THREE TIERS OF AVATARS
‣ Tier 3: EPIC AVATARS
You don’t always need these, but if you can get them 	you’ve
branded yourself yet again. 	You create a POSSE!
‣ menopausal women over 50: “silver sages”,
“menowarriors” (aggressive), “menogoddesses” (soft)
‣ 	Men over 35 who want to lose weight: “fatburners” (simple, but
a big desire!), “sixpackers” (another big desire), “waistline
warriors” (appeals to combat)
‣ people who spend more than $1000/day on Facebook:
“Facebookers”, “Adders” (cute), “ROIers” (again, presup)
‣ frequent air travelers: “world travelers”, “global citizens”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 20 --

EXAMPLES OF GREAT S.I. AVATARS
‣ Digital Marketers 	(simple, but damn effective)
‣ Coffee Drinkers 	(pro musician’s bass player posse)
‣ 	10%ers 	(personal development)
‣ Emailers 	(yours truly)
‣ Lovelighters 	(spiritual dating site)
‣ Iron Warriors 	(fitness)
‣ Supers 	(Internet Marketing SuperFriends; already a great name)
‣ CopyPro 	(yours truly again ; )
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 20 --

THESE ARE ALL AVATARS THAT MOST
WANT TO JOIN AS A CLUB
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 20 --

PROBLEMS VS COMPLAINTS
‣ People have problems 	that they complain about.
‣ 	Complaints are often 	wants in reverse.
‣ A more superficial (but important) bitchfest about a particular pain,
problem, or issue
‣ Example: 	your problem may be 	Blood Factor X, but you may not be
aware of it, and you complain about “getting hot all the time”,
“being easily winded”, or “being unable to lose weight”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 20 --

PRIMARY VS SECONDARY COMPLAINTS
‣ CopyPro uses “complaints” rather than problems for 	top of mind
impact.
‣ 	We later 	brand 	the actual problem underneath it all as what we call
The Primary Cause 	(Covered in Week 3.)
‣ Primary complaints 	are the #1 thing your customer is bitching
about when it comes to your niche. It’s the first thing out of their
mouths.
‣ Secondary complaints 	are all the things they may or may NOT
know go along with their primary complaint.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 20 --

PRIMARY VS SECONDARY COMPLAINTS
‣ CopyPro uses [verb] [pronoun] [primary complaint] as our basic
formula. This makes it easy to create and keeps data consistent (as
you’ll soon see, that’s important.) Pronouns are optional.
‣ 	Examples:
‣ losing money on Facebook
‣ packing on belly fat
‣ 	hiding your desire for connection
‣ When you start thinking 	verb first, 	you write better complaints.
The better the verb, the more visceral the complaint will resonate.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 20 --

PRIMARY VS SECONDARY COMPLAINTS
‣ The same [verb] [pronoun] [primary complaint] works for secondary
complaints. They should always relate back to the primary.
‣ 	Examples:
‣ losing money on Facebook: 	adding stress to your marriage,
spending a fortune on ‘guru’ courses
‣ packing on belly fat: 	looking at your clothes than no longer fit,
getting concerned looks from friends, comfort-eating in hiding
‣ 	hiding your desire for connection: 	letting yourself go,
entertaining thoughts of cheating, gazing at other men/women
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 20 --

PUTTING IT ALL TOGETHER
‣ “When you’re 	packing on belly fat 	everything feels uncomfortable.
Perhaps you’re getting concerned looks from friends. Maybe even
comfort-eating at night…”
‣ 	You can also use secondary complaints as 	bullets:
‣ • You’re getting concerned looks from your friends…
• You’re even “comfort-eating at night, often alone and hiding…
‣ And as 	teasers:
‣ 	Getting concerned looks from friends? Comfort-eating at night?
Then you’re 	packing on belly fat 	…and it’s getting worse.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
